entries.title: Adolescence Is Old. Teenagers Are New.
entries.rawOfficialText:
In 2001, millennial pop star Britney Spears sang that she was “not a girl, not yet a woman”. In other words, she was an adolescent. Actually, she was 20 at the time—no longer a teenager, but well below the age at which our frontal lobes stop developing. With your team, explore the history of teenagers and the related terms below. If adolescence has always been a developmental stage in both humans and many other animals, why is the idea of teenagers so new?
prefrontal cortex | neural pruning | risk-taking | neophilia | differentiation

links.label: UCLA - adolescence across the animal kingdom
Scientific American - The Amazing Teen Brain
Frontiers for Young Minds - Brain pruning / adolescence

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The UCLA article and podcast are not just support sources: they change the way the entry should be taught. They show adolescence as an evolutionary and cross-species stage of exploration, learning, dispersal, status sensitivity, risk, and plasticity. That means adolescence should not be framed only as a modern human problem or a defective half-adult brain.
The Scientific American link is central for the keyword slides. It explains the adolescent brain as adaptable and still under construction, with the prefrontal cortex maturing later than reward and emotion systems. This helps students understand risk-taking as a mismatch and an opportunity, not just stupidity.
The KidsHealth brain-development page is useful for visuals because it provides teen-friendly diagrams of the prefrontal cortex and related brain areas. It should guide image choice for the prefrontal cortex slide.
The raw Britney Spears line should be used as a hook only: she was 20, so the entry is deliberately separating teenager from adolescent. The real lesson is that adolescence is biological and developmental, while teenager is a modern social/cultural label.
entries.studentExplanation:
Adolescence is not just a modern human invention. The UCLA links show that many animals also pass through a risky, flexible learning stage between childhood and adulthood, when they explore, leave safe places, learn social rules, and test themselves. The newer part is the teenager as a social identity: a visible age group shaped by school, youth culture, spending money, music, media, and modern expectations. Even the famous idea that the brain is “finished at 25” is too simple: brain development is gradual, uneven, and shaped by experience.

The notes add an important correction to the “brain finished at 25” shortcut: adolescent development involves pruning, integration, segregation, white matter connections, and social learning across a flexible range, not one magic birthday. The modern teenager is newer than adolescence itself because school, consumer culture, music, media, and youth identity made that stage visible. Use this to connect biology, language, and culture: what transition is happening, when society recognizes it, how people perform or resist maturity, and why growing up rarely matches one clean age line.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this separates three ideas students often mix together: puberty, adolescence, and teenager identity. At the section level, it opens Going Pains by showing that growing up is biological, social, linguistic, and cultural all at once. At the year-theme level, adolescence is a classic threshold: a person is no longer simply a child, but not yet fully settled into adult roles.
entries.takeaway: Adolescence is ancient, but teenage identity is modern
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether adolescence should be understood mainly as biology, culture, language, or a changing social identity.
entries.counterargument: Biology matters, but teenage identity is also shaped by school, media, economics, family, and law.

channelVideos.title: Sarah-Jayne Blakemore: The mysterious workings of the adolescent brain

entries.title: Growing Up Has Different Names
entries.rawOfficialText: The word “adolescent” comes from a Latin word meaning “to grow toward”. But “teenager” relies on a quirk of the English language—that the numbers 11 and 12 have their own unique words, while 13 through 19 are compound words that combine a single-digit number and a suffix meaning “ten” (four-teen, six-teen, and so on). How do other languages refer to adolescents and teenagers—and does your own first language differentiate between these two terms? What are their different connotations? Do cultures with languages that use a -ten suffix for the numbers 11 and 12 define the “teenage” years differently? For instance, in Spanish, the -ten suffix only comes into play at 16—diez (10) y seis (6); do children only become teenagers at 16 in Spanish-speaking countries?
entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
This raw entry is mostly linguistic, so the key content is inside the wording itself. “Adolescent” comes from a growth-oriented Latin root, while “teenager” depends on a number pattern. Students should see that a word can define a life stage differently even when it points to similar ages.
The Spanish example is especially useful because it shows that English -teen does not map perfectly onto other languages. Spanish has special words for 11-15 and dieci- forms beginning at 16, but Spanish-speaking cultures do not wait until 16 to recognize adolescence.
The flashcards should not just translate “teenager.” They should compare whether a language frames the stage as age, growth, youth, puberty, or social identity.
entries.studentExplanation:
The word adolescent points toward growth: it comes from a root meaning to grow or grow toward maturity. The word teenager works differently: it depends on the English number pattern from thirteen to nineteen. That means the two words overlap, but they do not carry the same feeling. Adolescent sounds developmental or scientific; teenager sounds more social and cultural. Other languages divide the age range differently, which reminds us that life stages are partly biological and partly shaped by language.

Make language part of the evidence. “Adolescent” points toward growth and maturity, while “teenager” depends on English number words; other languages sort youth, puberty, and social age in different ways. That means the category is partly biological, partly linguistic, and partly cultural. Use this to connect biology, language, and culture: what transition is happening, when society recognizes it, how people perform or resist maturity, and why growing up rarely matches one clean age line.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students notice that words do not just label growing up; they shape how people imagine it. At the section level, it adds language and culture to the science of adolescence. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds because language is one way societies draw fuzzy borders between child, youth, and adult.
entries.takeaway: Life stages are shaped by biology and language
examples: Spanish: adolescente and the number puzzle
Mandarin Chinese and Arabic: youth more than “teen”
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating how much words and languages shape the boundaries between childhood, youth, and adulthood.
entries.counterargument: Language shapes categories, but people can still recognize adolescence even when their language divides ages differently.

entries.title: Rites, Rules, and the Moment You “Become” an Adult
entries.rawOfficialText:
Many cultures hold special ceremonies to celebrate the passage from the dependency of childhood to the responsibilities of adulthood. With your team, explore the following rites of passage and consider: what is the difference between a cultural rite, such as a quinceañera, and a secular one, like graduating from high school?
Sweet sixteens | Fiesta de Quinceañera | Vision quests | Guan Li | Seijin no Hi | Bar and Bat Mitzvahs | Walkabout | Khatam al-Quran | debuts | high school graduation | driver’s license | first paycheck | national service | voting | moving out
entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Britannica/van Gennep framework should shape this entry: rites of passage are not random celebrations; they organize movement from one status to another through separation, transition/liminality, and incorporation. This links directly to the year theme and to liminal thresholds.
The raw list deliberately mixes traditional, religious, public, legal, and everyday rites. The slideshow should keep that variety instead of making every example look like a birthday party.
The three Wikimedia visual links matter because rites of passage are highly visual. Quinceañera, Seijin no Hi, and Bar/Bat Mitzvah examples will help students see how clothing, ceremony, public recognition, and family/community presence make transition visible.
Walkabout needs especially careful handling. The slide should avoid treating it as a generic adventure or tourist myth; it should be framed respectfully as a concept often simplified or misrepresented in outside sources.
entries.studentExplanation:
Growing up is usually gradual, but many communities create special moments that say: now something has changed. Some rites of passage are cultural or religious, like a quinceañera, Seijin no Hi, or bar/bat mitzvah. Others are secular or legal, like graduation, voting, a driver’s license, national service, or moving out. The important point is that a rite turns a slow and messy transition into a visible social moment that other people recognize.

The extra sources help students ask what the ceremony is doing socially. A quinceanera, Seijin no Hi, bar or bat mitzvah, vision quest, graduation, driver’s license, or national service does not cause maturity by itself; it gives a community a visible moment when it agrees to treat a person differently. Use this to connect biology, language, and culture: what transition is happening, when society recognizes it, how people perform or resist maturity, and why growing up rarely matches one clean age line.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows that adulthood is not only biology; it is also marked by symbols, ceremonies, rights, and responsibilities. At the section level, it gives a social answer to the science of adolescence: rites of passage make change legible. At the year-theme level, rites of passage are literal threshold events between one status and another.
entries.takeaway: Communities give visible shape to uncertain transitions
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether rites of passage create adulthood or simply give public shape to gradual change.
entries.counterargument: Rituals can mark a threshold, but they do not instantly create maturity, responsibility, or adult confidence.

entries.title: Growing Up Is Messy
entries.rawOfficialText:
A ceremony offers a definite moment: now you are a man, or woman, or more broadly an adult. But the transition to adulthood is rarely a single, clean step; more often, it is a series of stumbles. The following works explore the messy middle years, where childhood collides with the expectations of tradition, society, and self-identity. As you explore them, consider: is adulthood a destination you reach or a mask you learn to wear?
Gwendolyn Brooks | “We Real Cool” (1963) | Virginia Driving Hawk Sneve | “The Medicine Bag” (1975) | Carol Ann Duffy | “In Mrs. Tilscher’s Class” (1990) | Joss Whedon | “Where Do We Go From Here?” (2001) | Karen Russell | “Lucy's Home for Girls Raised by Wolves” (2006) | Ruth B | “Lost Boy” (2015) | Major Jackson | “Let Me Begin Again” (2021)

links.label: Virginia Driving Hawk Sneve - The Medicine Bag
Carol Ann Duffy - In Mrs. Tilscher’s Class
Joss Whedon - Where Do We Go From Here?
Karen Russell - St. Lucy’s Home for Girls Raised by Wolves
Major Jackson - Let Me Begin Again

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The links are the actual raw content here. The entry has little meaning without the works, because each work gives a different model of the messy middle: rebellion, inherited responsibility, classroom innocence fading, uncertainty after conflict, forced adaptation, escapist belonging, and restart after damage.
We Real Cool should be taught as compressed teenage bravado racing toward consequences. The shortness and repeated “we” make identity feel collective, performative, and fragile.
The Medicine Bag and St. Lucy’s Home for Girls Raised by Wolves are crucial because they complicate growth: becoming older may mean carrying heritage, adapting under pressure, or being split between worlds, not simply “finding yourself.”
In Mrs. Tilscher’s Class should anchor the transition from childhood security to adolescent awareness. It is especially useful because the imagery turns classroom comfort into heat, curiosity, and storm-like change.
The video/song links should remain in the app as media content, not citations. Where Do We Go From Here? and Lost Boy let students hear uncertainty and escape rather than only read about them.
entries.studentExplanation:
The works in this entry do not present adulthood as one clean step. They show rebellion, embarrassment, inherited responsibility, classroom innocence fading, forced adaptation, escape, and the wish to begin again. Together, they make adolescence feel like a messy middle space where childhood and adulthood overlap. The linked poems, stories, and songs are not optional support: they are the content students need to explore in order to understand the entry.

Use the literature and songs as different models of messiness rather than as a single message. Brooks, Sneve, Duffy, Miranda, Springsteen, and the other works show rebellion, shame, family duty, classroom innocence, performance, and escape, so students should ask what each text thinks growing up costs. Use this to connect biology, language, and culture: what transition is happening, when society recognizes it, how people perform or resist maturity, and why growing up rarely matches one clean age line.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students see that art often captures growing up better than a definition can. At the section level, it gives emotional depth to Going Pains: growing up is something people feel, perform, resist, and survive. At the year-theme level, the entry asks whether adulthood is a destination someone reaches or a role someone slowly learns to wear.
entries.takeaway: Adulthood can feel like roles learned through mistakes
examples: “Lucy's Home for Girls Raised by Wolves” (2006)
Where Do We Go From Here?
St. Lucy’s Home for Girls Raised by Wolves
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether art captures adolescence better than definitions, ceremonies, or science alone.
entries.counterargument: Art reveals emotional truth, but it cannot replace historical, cultural, or psychological context.

entries.title: “Adulting”: Acting Like a Grown-Up
entries.rawOfficialText: Millennials—people born between about 1981 and 1995—gave us side parts, skinny jeans, and “adulting”. This term transformed adulthood into a verb, something you do (often with great effort) rather than something you simply are. Whether going to bed early, scheduling a dentist appointment, or buying a vacuum cleaner, describing your actions as “adulting” implies you are just role-playing as a grown-up. Consider with your team: is being an adult a specific feeling or just a collection of habits and responsibilities? When do you think you will feel like an adult?
entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Time article must be treated as core content because it explains the cultural logic of “adulting”: the speaker performs adult tasks while keeping ironic distance from being a “real adult.” The word is funny because it turns adulthood into an action rather than a settled identity.
This entry should connect to the app’s year theme through performance and thresholds. “Adulting” makes adulthood feel like a series of repeatable tasks: paying bills, scheduling appointments, sleeping responsibly, buying practical objects, and managing everyday life.
The entry is strongest when it does not mock young adults. It should show that adulting language reveals real uncertainty about when adulthood begins in contemporary life.
entries.studentExplanation:
Adulting turns adulthood into a verb: something you do, often awkwardly, instead of something you simply are. The Time link explains why the joke works: when people say they are “adulting,” they often mean they are doing grown-up tasks while still feeling a little distant from the identity of a “real adult.” Paying bills, making appointments, cooking, cleaning, buying practical things, or going to bed early can all feel like performances of adulthood before adulthood feels natural.

The useful detail is that “adulting” names a gap between task and identity. Paying bills, scheduling appointments, cooking, cleaning, or making practical purchases can make someone look adult from the outside while still feeling like rehearsal on the inside. Use this to connect biology, language, and culture: what transition is happening, when society recognizes it, how people perform or resist maturity, and why growing up rarely matches one clean age line.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand why adulthood can feel like a performance. At the section level, it modernizes Going Pains by showing how culture jokes about the gap between responsibility and identity. At the year-theme level, adulting is a liminal habit: a person is doing adult things while still asking whether they have really arrived.
entries.takeaway: Adulthood may be performed before it feels real
examples: Adulting turns adulthood into a verb
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether adulthood is an identity, a feeling, or a repeated performance of responsibilities.
entries.counterargument: Adulting language can reveal real uncertainty, but it may also exaggerate how new or unique modern adulthood difficulties are.

entries.title: The Last Threshold
entries.rawOfficialText:
Life is a journey—but does it end with a final port of call or a temporary layover? Explore the following works and discuss with your team: how do they handle the idea of life approaching its last liminal moment?
Rumi | “A stone I died” (c. 13th century) | John Donne | “Death Be not Proud” (1633) | Alfred Lord Tennyson | “Ulysses” (1842) | Emily Dickinson | “Because I could not stop for Death” (1890) | Rabindranath Tagore | “Gitanjali 95” (1913) | Dmitri Shostakovich | 15th Symphony: Fourth Movement (1972) | Thich Nhat Hanh | “Please Call Me by My True Names” (1976) | David Berman | “A Letter from Isaac Asimov to his Wife Janet, Written on His Deathbed” (1999) | Linkin Park | “In the End” (2000)

links.label: Emily Dickinson - “Because I could not stop for Death”
Thich Nhat Hanh - “Please Call Me by My True Names”

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
This entry widens Going Pains from the painful threshold of adolescence to the final threshold of life. The links are the content: each poem, song, letter, and symphony gives a different answer to whether death is an ending, crossing, transformation, defiance, or continuation.
Rumi and Tagore are useful for death as transformation or continuity. Donne and Linkin Park are more confrontational: Donne directly challenges death’s power, while Linkin Park turns effort and time into loss and frustration.
Tennyson’s Ulysses and Dickinson’s carriage poem are both journey-based, but they handle the last threshold differently: restless courage versus calm, strange transport. These should be paired in the student explanation or slideshow.
Thich Nhat Hanh’s poem adds an important non-Western and non-individualistic dimension: identity is not a single fixed self, but part of a larger web of suffering, compassion, and continuity.
Shostakovich and the music examples are crucial because they show that last thresholds can be expressed without literal explanation. Music can make the end feel suspended, haunted, repetitive, or unresolved.
entries.studentExplanation:
This entry moves from growing up to the final threshold: the approach of death. The linked works do not agree on what death means. Rumi imagines transformation, Donne challenges death’s power, Tennyson’s Ulysses keeps pushing toward one more voyage, Dickinson turns death into a calm carriage ride, Tagore treats death as another meeting with the unknown, Thich Nhat Hanh expands identity beyond one self, and Linkin Park makes time and effort feel fragile. Together, the works ask whether death is a final destination, a crossing, a transformation, or an unresolved mystery.

The poems and songs should be treated as competing maps of death: Rumi imagines transformation, Donne argues against death’s authority, Tennyson keeps pushing forward, Dickinson turns death into a carriage ride, Tagore frames it as encounter, and Thich Nhat Hanh challenges the idea of a single self ending. Use this to connect biology, language, and culture: what transition is happening, when society recognizes it, how people perform or resist maturity, and why growing up rarely matches one clean age line.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students compare artistic ways of imagining death. At the section level, it gives Going Pains a powerful final turn: the section begins with adolescence as a first unstable threshold and ends with death as the last threshold. At the year-theme level, it connects to endings, journeys, and liminality because the end of life is also imagined as a passage, port, layover, or transformation.
entries.takeaway: Art imagines death as ending, passage, and meaning
examples: “Because I could not stop for Death” (1890)
“Please Call Me by My True Names” (1976)
“A Letter from Isaac Asimov to his Wife Janet, Written on His Deathbed” (1999)
Emily Dickinson — “Because I could not stop for Death”
Thich Nhat Hanh — “Please Call Me by My True Names”
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether death should be imagined as an ending, a crossing, a transformation, or an unresolved mystery.
entries.counterargument: Art can make death thinkable, but different traditions and individuals may disagree deeply about what the final threshold means.

visualSections.title: Works slideshow: the last liminal moment
